sitting_overview: An urgent question on Government licences permitting imports of diesel and jet fuel refined from Russian crude drew an apology from the Trade Minister. Oral questions to the Technology Secretary covered the protection of children online, and the fifth day of the King's Speech debate, on defence, included Wes Streeting's first Commons speech since he resigned from the Cabinet.

highlights.summary: Wes Streeting (LAB), in his first Commons speech since resigning from the Cabinet, said the Government is losing the fight against nationalism and risks handing power to Reform.
highlights.key_quote: I left the Government because we are in the fight of our lives against nationalism, and it is a fight that we are currently losing. Unless we change course, we risk handing the keys of No. 10 to Reform, and I do not want that on our consciences.
highlights.analysis: Streeting spoke from the backbenches days after resigning from the Cabinet. He argued for patriotism over nationalism and said the Government must change course or risk losing to Reform. The speech drew praise across the House, including from the Conservative Mark Pritchard.

debate_map.quote: Bankrupting the country has consequences.
His speech and its content were, I am afraid, not as germane as the contribution of the hon. Member for Slough (Mr Dhesi), who chairs the Defence Committee.
In 21 years in this House, I think his was one of the best speeches I have heard.

highlights.summary: The Minister for Trade, Chris Bryant, apologised at the despatch box for the Government's handling of new licences covering fuel refined from Russian crude.
highlights.key_quote: The one bit of the right hon. Gentleman's remarks that I completely agree with is that we have handled this clumsily. That is entirely my fault, and I apologise to all hon. Members
highlights.analysis: The urgent question, granted in response to the Conservative MP Andrew Bowie, concerned trade licences permitting imports of diesel and jet fuel refined from Russian crude. The Minister for Trade, Chris Bryant, apologised for the handling of the announcement and said the measure tightens sanctions, through a new ban on third-country Russian-refined oil, rather than easing them. He said the UK had moved more slowly than the EU, and that responsibility split between the Foreign Office and his department had caused confusion.

debate_map.quote: It is an absolute disgrace. They should all be utterly ashamed of themselves.
this is more than just a redirection of policy; this is a betrayal of Ukraine.
While the Government are denying a licence to drill in the North sea, they are, unfortunately, giving Putin a licence to kill.
does he agree with me that this complex issue has been handled very, very clumsily, to put it mildly?

highlights.summary: Tanmanjeet Singh Dhesi (LAB), who chairs the Defence Committee, said NATO allies are questioning whether Britain can meet its commitments while the Defence Investment Plan stays unpublished.
highlights.key_quote: The US is carrying out a NATO audit, and it is looking hard at us. Our European partners are questioning whether we can deliver on the commitments we have made to NATO as part of the defence planning process, and whether we continue to deserve our leadership role in NATO.
highlights.analysis: Dhesi chairs the Defence Committee and spoke from the Government's own benches. The promised Defence Readiness Bill was absent from the King's Speech, and the Defence Investment Plan, due last autumn, remains unpublished. Earlier in the debate the Minister, Luke Pollard, declined to give a publication date, saying he would not provide a running commentary on it.

debate_map.quote: It is now 10 months late, so when the Minister responds, can he tell us exactly when we are going to get the defence investment plan?
I am not going to give a running commentary on the DIP. We are working flat out to deliver it and it will be published when it is ready.
Let us get the defence investment plan published right away—that is really important.

highlights.summary: The Technology Secretary, Liz Kendall, said the online-safety consultation is weighing overnight curfews, limits on addictive features and restrictions on AI chatbots, alongside a possible under-16 social media ban.
highlights.key_quote: our consultation is looking at not just whether there should be a ban on social media for under-16s but a whole range of other issues, including whether platforms should be required to switch off addictive features, whether there should be mandatory overnight curfews, whether there should be age or other restrictions on artificial intelligence chatbots
highlights.analysis: During departmental questions, the Technology Secretary set out the scope of the consultation on children growing up online: addictive design, overnight curfews, restrictions on AI chatbots, and stronger age verification. She said the Government would publish proposals before the summer and legislate, if required, by the end of the year.

debate_map.quote: Can the Secretary of State reassure the House that the Government's consultation on banning social media for under-16s will result in decisive action
it is time to legislate to ensure that they take active steps to protect our children online
Does the Secretary of State regret having been forced into a ban on social media for under-16s

highlights.summary: The Prime Minister, Keir Starmer, announced a 12-month road-tax holiday for hauliers and an extension of the fuel duty freeze, a day before the Chancellor's statement.
highlights.key_quote: I can announce today that we are giving our hauliers a 12-month road tax holiday, helping to keep prices down, and we are backing drivers by extending the freeze in fuel duty for the rest of the year.
highlights.analysis: Answering a question on cost-of-living pressures, the Prime Minister announced the two measures at the despatch box. The Leader of the Opposition, Kemi Badenoch, called the fuel-duty extension a reversal of the position he set out in March. He said the Chancellor would announce further action the next day.

debate_map.quote: What reassurance can the Prime Minister offer my constituents that this Government will tackle household bills and business cost pressures?
we asked him about this on 11 March and 25 March and he said he was not going to do it, so I thank him for the U-turn

highlights.constituency: Newcastle upon Tyne Central and West
highlights.summary: Chi Onwurah (LAB), who chairs the Science and Technology Committee, said engagement-driven digital advertising is the root of online harms and called for it to be regulated.
highlights.key_quote: The original sin underpinning many of today's digital harms is engagement-driven digital advertising. That is what incentivised the platforms to hook young people on infinite scrolling
highlights.analysis: Onwurah said the business model, advertising sold against engagement, is what drives platforms to design for compulsion, and noted that her Committee had called for digital advertising to be regulated almost a year earlier. The Minister, Liz Kendall, agreed and pointed to a cross-Government digital advertising taskforce.

debate_map.quote: Is it not the case that big tech companies are not paying taxes to this country and are avoiding substantive regulation, and that the Government now believe that big tech is too big to fail?
My hon. Friend is right: follow the money.

highlights.summary: Mike Martin (LD), a former Army officer, said the UK is down to five operational frigates, and cited a NATO exercise in which four drone operators wiped out a British brigade.
highlights.key_quote: During a recent NATO training exercise, a British brigade was effectively wiped out by four Ukrainian drone operators. That is not an indictment of our soldiers, but a reflection of modern warfare.
highlights.analysis: Martin cited specific figures: the UK is down to five operational frigates after two withdrawals this year; France keeps about 80% of its escort vessels available against the UK's 50%; and three Glasgow build slots have gone to Norway. The Minister, Luke Pollard, had said the Government is investing more than £270 billion in defence over this Parliament.

debate_map.quote: this Government are investing in hard power—investing over £270 billion in defence over this Parliament.
When we took the Falkland Islands, we had 53 frigates and destroyers available to us. We could not do that today.

highlights.summary: Laura Kyrke-Smith (LAB) described a constituent's 12-year-old son being groomed by an AI chatbot; the Prime Minister said he would look at age-related restrictions on children's use of chatbots.
highlights.key_quote: The chatbot turned him against his school and his parents, made sexual advances, and even suggested that the bot and the boy might meet in the afterlife.
highlights.analysis: Kyrke-Smith said current protections are inadequate. The Prime Minister called the account deeply concerning and said the Government would look at age-related bans, limits on children's use of chatbots and stronger parental controls, adding that he was meeting parents and civil society groups that afternoon.

debate_map.quote: we obviously have to look at what further measures we can take, including age-related bans, restricting children's use of chatbots and stronger parental controls.

highlights.summary: Anneliese Dodds (LAB) called on the Government to review its voluntary approach to AI safety, noting that one model produced more than 3 million sexualised images despite pre-release testing.
highlights.key_quote: The AI Security Institute's pre-release analysis of AI models did not prevent Grok's production of over 3 million sexualised images. Even Steve Bannon, of all people, is now calling for mandatory pre-release vetting of AI models, so has the time come for the UK to review our voluntary approach?
highlights.analysis: Dodds, a former party chair, said voluntary pre-release testing has failed. The Minister, Kanishka Narayan, did not commit to mandatory vetting, replying that some of Grok's outputs were already illegal and that the Government has ongoing conversations on further policy.

debate_map.quote: We will continue to enforce the law very robustly, and of course we have ongoing conversations on further policy.
what engagement do the Government have with those frontier companies, and particularly Anthropic?

highlights.summary: Graham Leadbitter (SNP), leading an adjournment debate, said his constituency has lost 77% of its bank branches since 2015 and that the criteria for approving shared banking hubs do not fit rural areas.
highlights.key_quote: In 2015, Moray West, Nairn and Strathspey had 22 bank branches. Just 11 years later, the number has dropped to just six in the latest data from January this year; that is a 77% reduction.
highlights.analysis: MPs from several parties joined the debate. Leadbitter said the criteria used by Link to recommend shared banking hubs, which weigh branch numbers, population and retail-centre size, do not reflect rural geography or the needs of cash-reliant businesses. The Economic Secretary, Rachel Blake, pointed to the Government's independent review of access to banking services, due to report in October 2026.

debate_map.quote: I have had 11 banks close in my constituency, and seeing the decline of banks in rural areas has been excessively worrying and frustrating.
For nine months I have been trying to get a banking hub in Ammanford
in its assessment for the Bitterne application, Link included bus times that simply did not exist on any timetable anywhere.
it would be premature to conclude that all people and communities are receiving support with their banking needs.

highlights.summary: Calum Miller (LD) said a brook in his constituency has the highest recorded level of PFAS 'forever chemicals' in the country, and that the Environment Agency has no plan to monitor it regularly.
highlights.key_quote: The BBC has reported today that the concentrations are 47,000 times higher than the environmental quality standard. However, the Environment Agency has no plan for regular monitoring.
highlights.analysis: Miller said Gallos brook in his constituency has the highest recorded concentration of PFAS 'forever chemicals' in the country, and described a constituent whose animals had died and who feared contaminating neighbours with home-grown vegetables. The Prime Minister agreed to arrange a meeting with the Environment and Health Secretaries, but gave no commitment on monitoring.

debate_map.quote: Yes, I will, and I thank the hon. Gentleman for raising the issue. I will ensure that the detail is looked at, and that appropriate steps are taken.

highlights.summary: Shockat Adam (IND) challenged the 'welfare versus warfare' framing of the debate, arguing that a healthy, educated population and a strong military are connected, not rival, goals.
highlights.key_quote: Is it because we do not build a strong country by making poor people poorer, and we do not build a strong Army from an unhealthy, under-educated and economically broken population? The two things are not in competition; they are connected.
highlights.analysis: Several speakers had framed the choice as cutting welfare to fund defence. Adam rejected that framing, arguing that a healthy, educated population is itself a defence asset. He cited the Ajax, Bowman and Type 45 programmes to question where the real inefficiency lies.

debate_map.quote: We cannot defend Britain with an ever-expanding welfare budget.
I urge the Government to look carefully at our proposals on reducing the welfare bill.

highlights.summary: Claire Hanna (SDLP) challenged the Conservative case on Northern Ireland veterans, saying very few of the 300,000 who served under Operation Banner have faced judicial proceedings.
highlights.key_quote: Would he care to tell the House how many of the 300,000 soldiers who served under Operation Banner in Northern Ireland have faced judicial proceedings? It is in the very low two figures.
highlights.analysis: The Conservative amendment to the King's Speech opposes the Northern Ireland Troubles Bill, arguing it exposes veterans to what the Conservatives call lawfare. Hanna said no Northern Ireland party or victims' group supports the Conservative approach. Cartlidge said it was unlikely new cases would lead to prosecutions but that reopening them would damage armed forces morale.

debate_map.quote: it is extremely unlikely that any new cases would lead to prosecutions. It is, however, certain that were this process to recommence, it would damage the morale of our armed forces at a time of war on two fronts
The vexatious nature of these complaints will, of course, eventually drag them back into the courts.
it would be legal to have a statute of limitation provided that it was coupled with a truth recovery process

highlights.summary: Julian Lewis (CON), a former Defence Committee chair, argued that peacetime defence spending is cheap insurance, since a full-scale war would force spending to 30% or 40% of GDP.
highlights.key_quote: If, by investing in defence in peacetime, we can deter a potential enemy from starting a war, we not only save the treasure; we save the blood that is otherwise shed so copiously.
highlights.analysis: Lewis argued that a full-scale war with a peer adversary would force defence spending to 30% or 40% of GDP, not 3% or 4%, so peacetime investment is the cheaper course. He compared it to pandemic preparedness, where capability is funded only once the crisis arrives.

debate_map.quote: freedom is the most expensive thing that we will ever try to own
the best way to have peace is to prepare for war.

highlights.debate: Growing up in the Online World: Consultation
highlights.summary: Fred Thomas (LAB) questioned the design of the online-safety consultation, saying it has no separate channel for evidence from medical experts as distinct from submissions by tech companies.
highlights.key_quote: There is also an absence of any clear mechanism for considering evidence from medical experts and frontline professionals separately from submissions from tech companies, which obviously have a commercial interest in opposing any restrictions.
highlights.analysis: Thomas welcomed the consultation but questioned its framing: questions that prompt respondents to weigh the benefits of social media, no separate channel for medical evidence, and the use of AI to analyse responses. The Technology Secretary replied that the Government was seeking the views of medical professionals, citing the Royal College of Psychiatrists and an expert panel.

debate_map.quote: We are actively seeking the views of medical professionals and parents.
they welcome the Government's consultation but are concerned about the timing

highlights.summary: Ian Byrne (LAB) said hunger in Britain is a political choice, and quoted a constituent who told him deprived communities should be called 'asset-stripped', not 'disadvantaged'.
highlights.key_quote: In Northumberland, one woman said something I will never, ever forget. She said, "Don't you dare call us disadvantaged; call us asset-stripped."
highlights.analysis: Byrne argued that hunger in Britain is a political choice rather than a matter of scarcity, drawing on five months of evidence-gathering across the country for his Right to Food campaign. He attributed the phrase 'asset-stripped' to a woman he met in Northumberland.

highlights.summary: Stuart Anderson (CON), a member of the NATO Parliamentary Assembly, said a NATO defence minister had told the forum last month that the UK is 'not even in the room'.
highlights.key_quote: in the same cross-party forum last month, a NATO Defence Minister—I will not say which country he was from—said, "With all due respect, the UK are not leading; they are not even in the room, and they are not funding as they were."
highlights.analysis: Anderson, a member of the NATO Parliamentary Assembly, contrasted the remark with a Latvian minister's praise of British leadership at the same forum in 2022. He did not name the country of the minister he quoted.

debate_map.quote: Our European partners are questioning whether we can deliver on the commitments we have made to NATO
NATO's Secretary-General is right to warn today that our alliance has an "unhealthy" reliance on one ally.

highlights.summary: Alec Shelbrooke (CON) said public scepticism about defence spending is itself a target of Russian information operations.
highlights.key_quote: They are building a psychological profile of what you are, and then they identify your psychological vulnerabilities. They then feed that line. Why? Because they are trying to get this country, among lots of other countries, not to see the need to invest in defence.
highlights.analysis: Shelbrooke said hostile states build psychological profiles of individuals and feed them messaging designed to erode support for defence spending, and that the debate the House was holding is itself such a target.

debate_map.quote: a lack or a void of information creates speculation and misinformation from our enemies

highlights.summary: Michelle Scrogham (LAB) said the Chief of the Defence Staff had told her the public is not told more about defence in order not to alarm them, and that she believes they should be.
highlights.key_quote: When I asked the Chief of the Defence Staff recently why we do not tell the public more, he said, "We don't want to alarm people." Does my hon. Friend agree that the public are grown-ups, and that they should probably be a little more alarmed than they already are?
highlights.analysis: Scrogham made the remark as a brief intervention in another member's speech. Several speakers in the debate said the public should be better informed about the threats facing the UK.

debate_map.quote: We must bring the British people with us on this journey and trust them with that information.
we need to engage the public in a national conversation about spending more on defence
The general public do not know the state of the British military

sections.summary: The sitting opened with prayers, followed by a notice from the Speaker that the ballot for private Members' Bills would be drawn the next morning.

Oral questions to the Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, Liz Kendall, and her ministers. Members questioned the Government on protecting children online, including the consultation on a possible under-16 social media ban, addictive platform design, online pornography and AI chatbots. They also raised digital inclusion, the regulation of digital advertising, and whether the UK should align with EU technology rules. The Government said it would publish proposals before the summer and legislate, if required, by the end of the year.

At Prime Minister's Questions, the Prime Minister, Keir Starmer, announced a 12-month road-tax holiday for hauliers and an extension of the fuel duty freeze. The main exchange, with the Leader of the Opposition, Kemi Badenoch, concerned Government licences permitting imports of fuel refined from Russian crude in third countries: she said they helped fund Russia's war, while the Prime Minister said they tightened sanctions rather than easing them. Backbench questions raised AI chatbot safety, PFAS contamination of waterways and a range of constituency matters.

An urgent question on the Government's decision to issue general trade licences for diesel and jet fuel refined from Russian crude oil in third countries. The Minister for Trade, Chris Bryant, apologised for the handling of the announcement and said the measure tightens sanctions rather than relaxing them, through a new ban on third-country Russian-refined oil. Opposition Members questioned the policy's consistency and its implications for support for Ukraine.

Jeremy Corbyn raised a point of order about the West Bank village of Khan al-Ahmar, asking how a Foreign Office Minister might be brought to the House to address the situation there. The Speaker said he expected a Government statement, and that an urgent question could be sought if one did not come.

The procedural resumption, for its fifth day, of the debate on the Humble Address, the Commons' formal reply thanking the Crown for the Gracious Speech. The day's substantive debate, on defence, follows under Defence Readiness.

The fifth day of the debate on the King's Speech, on the theme of defence. Members debated the level of UK defence spending and military capability, the delayed Defence Investment Plan, the state of the Royal Navy and Army, and Britain's standing within NATO. The debate also covered an Opposition amendment on the prosecution of Northern Ireland veterans, and ranged into national resilience and food poverty. It included the first Commons speech by Wes Streeting since his resignation from the Cabinet.
Three statutory instruments were approved without debate, covering controlled-drug precursors, international co-operation in criminal justice, and merchant shipping port-state control.
Julia Buckley presented a petition, signed by more than 10,000 people, in support of proposals to restore direct rail services linking Wrexham, Shropshire and the Midlands to London.

Charlie Dewhirst presented a petition, signed by 5,784 local residents, opposing the proposed closure of the care unit at Bridlington hospital and calling for services there to be retained and expanded.

An adjournment debate, led by Graham Leadbitter, on bank branch closures and access to banking services. Members from across the House described the loss of branches in rural and urban communities and criticised the criteria used to approve shared banking hubs. The Economic Secretary to the Treasury, Rachel Blake, pointed to the Government's independent review of access to banking services, due to report in October 2026.
